text: 5,6-Dichloro-2-methylbenzimidazole (DC-MBI), chemical formula C8H6Cl2N2, molecular weight 201.07, is a single-component hydrogen-bonded organic molecular crystal and an above-room-temperature ferroelectric. At room temperature it crystallizes in the non-centrosymmetric, polar orthorhombic space group Pca2_1 (No. 29), with unit cell a = 14.238 Å, b = 5.6866 Å, c = 10.398 Å, Z = 4. DC-MBI molecules link through intermolecular N-H...N hydrogen bonds into chains stacked along the b axis, with the polarity of each chain directed along the c axis; ferroelectricity arises from positional ordering of the bridging protons. In this work, variable-temperature powder and single-crystal X-ray diffraction combined with symmetry-adapted distortion-mode analysis showed that DC-MBI undergoes an unusual first-order, symmetry-lowering transition on warming from orthorhombic Pca2_1 (T below about 400 K) to monoclinic, still-polar Pc; second-harmonic-generation measurements confirmed the crystal remains non-centrosymmetric up to sublimation. No piezoelectric coefficient was measured, and the high room-temperature coercive field prevented ferroelectric hysteresis switching except at elevated temperature. The crystal structure (CCDC 909439) was determined by single-crystal X-ray diffraction at 295 K with an R factor of 4.27%, a density of 1.586 g/cm3, and a colorless block habit. The phase-transition study was reported by Liu et al. in the Journal of the American Chemical Society (2018); the structure and ferroelectricity were originally reported by Horiuchi et al. in Nature Communications (2012).
property_ref_title: Understanding the Behavior of the Above-Room-Temperature Molecular Ferroelectric 5,6-Dichloro-2-methylbenzimidazole Using Symmetry Adapted Distortion Mode Analysis
property_ref_journal: Journal of the American Chemical Society
